results.title: Hyposalivation and Xerostomia and Burning Mouth Syndrome: Medical Management.
results.abstract: In this chapter, the authors review the benefits of saliva and the destructive consequences of its loss. It is hoped that this will help their colleagues identify and treat patients before development of symptoms. Xerostomia is the subjective complaint of dry mouth or sensation of oral dryness. Hyposalivation is the actual decrease in measured salivary outflow. The authors discuss a compiled list of highly cited medications commonly used today that are linked with xerostomia and hyposalivation. There are numerous treatment modalities that are present, such as saliva substitutes, mouth rinses, sugar-free candy, and pilocarpine among others.
results.journal: Oral and maxillofacial surgery clinics of North America

results.title: Clinical characteristics and management outcome in the burning mouth syndrome. An open study of 130 patients.
results.abstract: Clinical characteristics and treatment responses were studied in 130 patients with burning mouth syndrome (BMS). Most patients were postmenopausal women, and the tongue was the most frequently afflicted site. Although 39% of the patients complained of dry mouth, no causative factors were evident. Therefore BMS is assumed to be a functional disorder. This was at least partially confirmed because the most effective management was in response to mood-altering drugs. From our data BMS appears to be a chronic condition with variations in symptoms among patients and without a predictable endpoint.
results.journal: Oral surgery, oral medicine, and oral pathology

results.title: Significance of saliva for the denture-wearing population.
results.abstract: This paper summarises a series of studies already published in German and presents new data related to the aetiology of the 'dry mouth' and its associated problems.

results.title: Conjunctival epithelium improvement after systemic pilocarpine in patients with Sjogren's syndrome.
results.abstract: To evaluate the effect of oral pilocarpine treatment on conjunctival epithelium of patients with Sjögren's syndrome (SS).
